facts: When two tectonic plates collide, the crust crumples and pushes upward, forming mountain ranges because the plates are too thick to slide under each other easily.
Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates sliding past each other get stuck and then suddenly slip, releasing stored energy as shaking; they don't happen when plates move smoothly.

Volcanoes commonly form at plate boundaries where one plate dives beneath another (subduction), because the sinking plate melts and creates magma that rises; a volcano is not a random burst from a flat plate interior.
Igneous rock forms when magma or lava cools and solidifies, not from compressed sediment or from heat-pressure change in solid rock.
Metamorphic rock is created when heat and pressure alter the minerals of an existing rock without melting it; for example, shale (sedimentary) turns into slate (metamorphic).

The rock cycle shows that any rock type can become another over time: for instance, sedimentary rock can be buried and heated to become metamorphic, which can then melt into magma to form igneous rock.
misconceptions: Students believe that earthquakes happen randomly anywhere on Earth, not realizing that most occur along tectonic plate boundaries where plates slide or collide.
Students think that all rocks form from cooled magma (igneous), so they assume sedimentary and metamorphic rocks are also from volcanoes.
Students believe that mountains form when the ground is pushed up from below by magma, rather than when tectonic plates collide and crumple the crust.
